title: Pentagon's $4B Space Tracking Deal and China's Nuclear Infrastructure Build
subhead: SpaceX wins major SB-AMTI contract as satellite imagery reveals China constructing vast launch-pad networks near its nuclear silo fields, while SoftBank announces Europe's largest AI infrastructure investment.

sections.heading: SpaceX Wins $4.16B for Space-Based Aircraft Tracking
sections.body:
The U.S. Space Force awarded SpaceX a $4.16 billion agreement to accelerate development of a space-based Air Moving Target Indicator (AMTI) sensor network, with an early capability targeted for 2028—years ahead of previous timelines that projected such systems for the 2030s. The Space-Based AMTI program aims to provide persistent, global tracking of airborne targets from orbit, fundamentally shifting surveillance away from traditional aircraft like the E-3 Sentry AWACS and E-7 Wedgetail.

The deal, described as a 'competitive Other Transaction Authority agreement' rather than a traditional contract, establishes SpaceX as a central player in U.S. military space architecture. The Space Force explicitly stated it will leverage a 'multi-vendor framework' and not rely on any single provider, though SpaceX's dominant launch capacity gives it substantial advantages.

This capability would enable the U.S. military to track aircraft globally and persistently from space, closing kill chains for long-range strikes even when traditional airborne surveillance assets are unavailable or threatened. The system complements ongoing work on space-based Ground Moving Target Indicator (GMTI) capabilities, together representing what TWZ described as potentially 'panoptic or near panoptic targeting and surveillance from space.'
sections.whyItMatters: This accelerates the shift of core surveillance missions from vulnerable aircraft to distributed satellite constellations, creating a more resilient targeting architecture while deepening SpaceX's role as an indispensable defense contractor.

sections.heading: China Builds Launch-Pad Network Near Nuclear Silos
sections.body:
Satellite imagery reviewed by Reuters reveals China is constructing a sprawling network of over 80 launch pads, bunkers, and communications nodes near its nuclear missile silo fields in Xinjiang. The facilities, centered on two octagon-shaped military installations built over the past six years, appear designed to ensure second-strike capability by protecting mobile missile launchers and supporting command-and-control operations.

The infrastructure includes hardened weapons storage areas, air-defense missile positions, and what analysts identify as possible electronic warfare and satellite communications facilities. Roads and conduits connect the pads to the octagon structures, with fiber-optic cables likely enabling rapid communication with launch units. Tong Zhao of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace noted the complex may be linked to 'C3—command, control, and communications' for nuclear operations at the Hami ICBM silo site.

The scale of construction has surprised analysts. Hans Kristensen of the Federation of American Scientists said: 'I've never seen anything quite like it. It's an extraordinary effort.' The network potentially sets China apart from other nuclear powers by combining silos with extensive defensive infrastructure rather than relying on numbers and hardening alone.
sections.whyItMatters: This represents a significant upgrade to China's nuclear posture, ensuring survivability of its deterrent through mobile launchers and layered defenses—a model that differs from U.S. and Russian approaches and signals Beijing's focus on maintaining credible retaliation capability.

sections.heading: Pentagon's AI Push Meets Military Caution
sections.body:
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth is pushing to 'rapidly evolve the military through AI', telling SpaceX employees in January he would reject any AI models 'that won't allow you to fight wars' and seeking systems 'without ideological constraints that limit lawful military applications.' The Pentagon has secured deals with eight AI companies including Google, OpenAI, and SpaceX for classified military work, replacing Anthropic after a bitter contract dispute.

But some military leaders are sounding notes of caution. Adm. Frank Bradley, head of U.S. Special Operations Command, said troops must be 'very careful about how we come to (AI's) employment and its inspiration into the delivery of lethality', emphasizing that humans must have confidence AI 'is going to deliver violence only where we intend it to be delivered.'

The clash over AI integration has played out publicly. After Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei refused to back down on safety concerns, the Pentagon labeled the company a 'supply chain risk', ending its $200 million defense contract. Anthropic sued, claiming illegal retaliation. Helen Toner of Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology noted the military approaches new technologies with significant caution: 'Commanders want their missions to succeed, which means both being able to create lethal effects at scale, and avoiding unintended effects like friendly fire, civilian casualties, or simply identifying targets incorrectly.'
sections.whyItMatters: The Pentagon is racing to deploy AI across military operations while wrestling with ethical constraints and reliability concerns—tension between rapid adoption and operational safety that will shape U.S. military capabilities for years.

sections.heading: AUKUS Launches Underwater Drone Project
sections.body:
The U.S., U.K., and Australia announced development of uncrewed undersea vehicle (UUV) technology to protect seabed infrastructure, conduct strikes, and perform surveillance—the first 'signature project' under AUKUS Pillar Two. The technology is expected to be operational by next year, with the U.K. contributing £150 million ($201 million).

UK Defence Secretary John Healey acknowledged criticism of AUKUS progress, stating: 'For too long in Aukus, we talked too much and delivered too little. That has now changed.' The project addresses growing threats to undersea cables and pipelines, with 60 undersea cables connecting the U.K. and Russian naval activity in UK waters increasing 30% over recent years.

The announcement comes amid questions about AUKUS's nuclear submarine timeline, with submarines not scheduled to be ready until the 2040s. U.S. Defense Secretary Hegseth confirmed plans to rotate U.S. and UK nuclear submarines through Australia remain 'still on track', with the first U.S. Navy personnel arriving later this year and HMAS Stirling expected to host rotational forces by end of 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: AUKUS is accelerating deliverable capabilities to counter criticism of slow progress, focusing on undersea infrastructure protection as Russia and China increasingly target cables—while the submarine program's long timeline remains a vulnerability.

sections.heading: SoftBank Bets $88B on France AI Data Hub
sections.body:
SoftBank Group will invest up to ¥14 trillion ($88 billion) to build data centers in France with total capacity expected to reach 5 gigawatts—among the largest AI infrastructure projects in Europe. The move reflects SoftBank's strategy to secure AI computing power outside the United States, according to Nikkei Asia.

The investment comes as European countries seek to develop domestic AI infrastructure and reduce dependence on U.S. cloud providers. France has positioned itself as Europe's AI hub, with the 5 gigawatt capacity representing substantial computing infrastructure for training large AI models.

SoftBank CEO Masayoshi Son has been expanding the company's AI investments globally, and the France project represents one of the largest single AI infrastructure commitments outside the U.S. market.
sections.whyItMatters: SoftBank's massive investment signals a shift in global AI infrastructure toward Europe, potentially giving the EU more autonomous computing capacity while reducing transatlantic dependency for training advanced models.

sections.heading: Europe's E-SAF Gets Military Boost from Iran War
sections.body:
Europe's struggling electro-Sustainable Aviation Fuel (e-SAF) sector is seeing renewed interest from military forces concerned about energy supply security following the Iran war and Strait of Hormuz disruption. The conflict has 'made the business case for e-SAF much stronger', according to Mariano Berkenwald of Ineratec, which operates Europe's only e-SAF production facility in Frankfurt.

E-SAF can reduce aviation emissions by 90% and—critically—can be produced entirely from European-sourced materials. This has attracted military attention: Germany's armed forces recently tested Ineratec's fuel, and other governments have expressed interest in building capacity. The fuel costs about 10 times more than kerosene, but militaries may pay a premium for supply security.

The EU has mandated 1.2% e-SAF blending by 2030 and 35% by 2050, but the sector was struggling with high costs and low investment. About 40 projects under development are stuck, unable to secure funding. The Iran war has shifted momentum, with the European Commission considering an EU financing mechanism for e-SAF.
sections.whyItMatters: Military interest in e-SAF for supply security could jumpstart a struggling green fuel sector—defense procurement may succeed where climate policy alone failed, creating European energy independence for aviation fuel.

sections.heading: Rare Earth Supply Chains One Year After China's Export Controls
sections.body:
A CSIS analysis one year after China's April 2025 rare earth export restrictions finds that despite the Trump-Xi trade truce, export flows remain volatile and U.S. imports have not recovered to pre-restriction levels. While European imports rebounded with a 60% year-over-year surge, U.S. imports fell 11% in November 2025, and certain critical minerals face severe shortages.

Chinese customs data show yttrium exports to the U.S. dropped from 333 tons in the eight months before restrictions to just 17 tons after. Aerospace manufacturers report rationing and potential production pauses. The analysis notes China remains an 'unreliable export partner to the United States during times of heightened geopolitical tensions.'

The U.S. has launched what CSIS calls 'the boldest domestic industrial policy in modern history', committing over $7.3 billion across five agencies. This includes a $400 million equity investment in MP Materials with a 10-year price floor at $110/kg, a $620 million loan to Vulcan Elements, and international investments including a 49% stake in a Saudi refinery and $565 million for Brazil's Serra Verde mine. Yet the analysis concludes that 'true resilience will be measured not by policy announcements or deployed capital, but by sustained output, diversified supply, and the ability to attract private investment.'
sections.whyItMatters: Despite massive U.S. investment, China's export controls continue to distort supply chains a year later—demonstrating that industrial policy alone cannot quickly replace deeply embedded supply chains, and geopolitical leverage persists.

bottomLine: The day's developments show the U.S. military racing to build space-based surveillance capabilities and AI-enabled systems while managing internal tensions over safety and ethics. China continues to harden its nuclear infrastructure and maintain rare earth leverage despite U.S. counter-efforts. AUKUS is delivering near-term underwater drone capabilities while the submarine program remains years away. And Europe is seeing major AI infrastructure investment from SoftBank while its green aviation fuel sector gets an unexpected boost from military supply security concerns after the Iran war.
sources: The War Zone, Defense News, AP News, BBC, Nikkei Asia, SCMP, France24/AFP, CSIS, Reuters
